section.title: The Cup and Judah's Plea
section.sectionRationale: Joseph springs his final test: his silver cup is hidden in Benjamin's sack, and the brothers are brought back as accused thieves. The moment that once destroyed Joseph, a favored son handed over, now returns, and Judah must choose. His plea is the moral summit of the whole account.
section.thesis: Joseph's last test exposes whether the brothers will again abandon a favored son; Judah answers by offering himself in Benjamin's place, proving that repentance has done its work.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the planted cup and the pursuit; the cup found and the brothers' collapse; and Judah's plea, offering himself as a slave in place of the boy.

units.label: The planted cup
units.note: why have you repaid good with evil

verses.text: Then Joseph instructed his steward: “Fill the men’s sacks with as much food as they can carry, and put each one’s silver in the mouth of his sack.
Put my cup, the silver one, in the mouth of the youngest one’s sack, along with the silver for his grain.” So the steward did as Joseph had instructed.
At daybreak, the men were sent on their way with their donkeys.
They had not gone far from the city when Joseph told his steward, “Pursue the men at once, and when you overtake them, ask, ‘Why have you repaid good with evil?
Is this not the cup my master drinks from and uses for {{nachash|divination}}? What you have done is wicked!’”
When the steward overtook them, he relayed these words to them.
“Why does my lord say these things?” they asked. “Your servants could not possibly do such a thing.
We even brought back to you from the land of Canaan the silver we found in the mouths of our sacks. Why would we steal silver or gold from your master’s house?
If any of your servants is found to have it, he must die, and the rest will become slaves of my lord.”
“As you say,” replied the steward. “But only the one who is found with the cup will be my slave, and the rest of you shall be free of blame.”

units.label: The cup found, the brothers fall
units.note: God has exposed the iniquity of your servants

verses.text: So each one quickly lowered his sack to the ground and opened it.
The steward searched, beginning with the oldest and ending with the youngest, and the cup was found in Benjamin’s sack.
Then they all tore their clothes, loaded their donkeys, and returned to the city.
When Judah and his brothers arrived at Joseph’s house, he was still there, and they fell to the ground before him.
“What is this deed you have done?” Joseph declared. “Do you not know that a man like me can surely divine the truth?”

“What can we say to my lord?” Judah replied. “How can we plead? How can we justify ourselves? God has exposed the {{avon|iniquity}} of your servants. We are now my lord’s slaves, both we and the one who was found with the cup.”
But Joseph replied, “Far be it from me to do this. The man who was found with the cup will be my slave. The rest of you may return to your father in peace.”

units.label: Judah's plea
units.note: let your servant remain in place of the boy

verses.text: Then Judah approached Joseph and said, “Sir, please let your servant speak personally to my lord. Do not be angry with your servant, for you are equal to Pharaoh himself.
My lord asked his servants, ‘Do you have a father or a brother?’
And we answered, ‘We have an elderly father and a younger brother, the child of his old age. The boy’s brother is dead. He is the only one of his mother’s sons left, and his father loves him.’
Then you told your servants, ‘Bring him down to me so that I can see him for myself.’
So we said to my lord, ‘The boy cannot leave his father. If he were to leave, his father would die.’
But you said to your servants, ‘Unless your younger brother comes down with you, you will not see my face again.’
Now when we returned to your servant my father, we relayed your words to him.
Then our father said, ‘Go back and buy us some food.’
But we answered, ‘We cannot go down there unless our younger brother goes with us. So if our younger brother is not with us, we cannot see the man.’
And your servant my father said to us, ‘You know that my wife bore me two sons.
When one of them was gone, I said: “Surely he has been torn to pieces.” And I have not seen him since.
Now if you also take this one from me and harm comes to him, you will bring my gray hair down to Sheol in sorrow.’
So if the boy is not with us when I return to your servant, and if my father, whose life is wrapped up in the boy’s life,
sees that the boy is not with us, he will die. Then your servants will have brought the gray hair of your servant our father down to Sheol in sorrow.
Indeed, your servant guaranteed the boy’s safety to my father, saying, ‘If I do not return him to you, I will bear the guilt before you, my father, all my life.’
Now please let your servant stay here as my lord’s {{eved|slave}} in place of the boy. Let him return with his brothers.
For how can I go back to my father without the boy? I could not bear to see the misery that would overwhelm him.”

nachash.gloss: To practice divination or read omens, here associated with a special cup. The claim fits Joseph's Egyptian disguise and heightens the drama, though his true insight comes from God, not omens. The word reminds us the brothers think they face a mysterious, all-seeing lord.

avon.gloss: Iniquity, guilt, or the punishment guilt deserves. Judah confesses that God has exposed the iniquity of the brothers, reaching past the cup to their old sin against Joseph. The word names the deep moral debt now surfacing in the light.

eved.gloss: A servant or slave. Judah offers to become the lord's eved in Benjamin's place, a stunning reversal for the man who once sold his brother into slavery. The word carries the whole weight of his substitution.

basic.context: As the brothers leave Egypt, Joseph has his own silver cup hidden in Benjamin's sack, then has them chased down as thieves. The scene deliberately recreates the old crisis: a favored son of Rachel is about to be lost. The key question is whether the brothers will abandon Benjamin as they once abandoned Joseph.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a carefully staged test within the narrative, and the reader knows the cup was planted. The main question is what Joseph is trying to reveal about his brothers' hearts. Judah's long speech answers it, showing that real change has taken place.
basic.originalLanguages: The cup is tied to nachash, divination, part of Joseph's Egyptian disguise. Judah confesses that God has exposed their avon, their iniquity, reaching back to their old sin. Then he offers to become an eved, a slave, in Benjamin's place, the very fate they once forced on Joseph.
basic.typology: Judah's offer to be enslaved so his brother and father can go free is a vivid picture of substitution. It foreshadows Jesus, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, who gives Himself into bondage and death so that others may be set free and brought home.
basic.christ: Judah stands in the place of the condemned so the beloved son can return to the father. This is the shape of the gospel: the innocent taking the guilty one's sentence, fulfilled when Christ takes our place.
basic.god: Judah says God has exposed their guilt. God is the one uncovering old sin and bringing it into the light, not to crush but to heal. His providence has arranged the whole test.
basic.crossReferences: Judah's substitution looks ahead to Genesis 45 and to Christ in Isaiah 53 and Romans 5. His plea shows the change hinted at in Genesis 38 and 43 come to full flower.
basic.oneStory: The brother who once sold a favored son now offers his own freedom to save one. God is knitting a broken family back together, preserving the messianic line of Judah and previewing the substitution at the heart of salvation.

context.literary: The climax of Joseph's testing and the longest speech in Genesis. The scene mirrors chapter 37 in reverse: again a beloved son of Rachel is threatened, again the brothers hold his fate, but this time Judah pleads for him. Torn clothes, the return to the city, and the falling before Joseph all echo earlier moments.
context.historical: A silver divining cup and a steward managing a great household fit an Egyptian setting. Theft from a ruler's house was a capital matter, and enslavement for debt or crime was a real and dreaded outcome, which sharpens the stakes of Judah's offer.
context.canonical: Judah, ancestor of David and of Christ, emerges here as a self-sacrificing intercessor. This chapter is a key witness to the transformation of the brothers and a crucial link in the line that leads to the Messiah, the greater substitute.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the brothers' repentance to its decisive proof through Judah's substitution, and to reveal how far God's providence has brought them from the men who sold Joseph. The narrator wants the reader to feel the reversal of chapter 37.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Joseph's staged test and Judah's plea; it does not commend deception as a general practice. The abiding truths are the reality of exposed guilt, the beauty of genuine repentance, and the power of substitutionary love.

debates.question: Was Joseph's use of a divining cup real divination or part of his disguise?

views.label: Disguise, not real omen reading
views.case: Joseph maintains his Egyptian cover; his insight into events comes from God and from having engineered the test, not from the cup itself.

views.label: A claim taken at face value
views.case: Some hold the text lets the brothers, and perhaps the reader, take the divination claim straightforwardly as part of an Egyptian ruler's practice, without endorsing it.

debates.bearing: Scripture elsewhere condemns divination; here the detail serves the disguise and the test, and the story's true source of knowledge is God's guidance of Joseph, not the cup.

study.originalLanguages: The cup is linked to nachash, divination, reinforcing Joseph's Egyptian mask, though his real knowledge is from God and from his own design. Judah confesses that God has exposed their avon, iniquity and the guilt it deserves, an admission that reaches beyond the cup to the buried sin against Joseph. Then Judah asks to remain as the lord's eved, a slave, in place of the boy, taking upon himself the very bondage once inflicted on his brother.

typology.type: Judah offering to be enslaved for Benjamin
typology.fulfillment: Christ, of Judah's line, who takes the place of the condemned to set the beloved free.
Benjamin spared through a substitute, as sinners are spared through Christ who bears their sentence.
The Spirit convicting of sin so that it may be confessed and forgiven in Christ.

study.christ: Judah's plea is one of the clearest Old Testament portraits of substitution. He steps between the beloved son and the sentence, offering to become a slave so Benjamin can return to the father, and so the father's heart is not broken. This is precisely the shape of the gospel: the guilty deserve the sentence, but another takes their place. Fittingly, it is Judah, the tribe from which the Messiah comes, who prefigures Christ, the innocent who is bound and condemned so that His people go free and are brought home to the Father.
study.god: God is named at the moral center of the chapter when Judah says God has exposed the iniquity of your servants. God is the one drawing hidden guilt into the open, not to destroy the brothers but to complete their repentance and reunite the family. Behind the planted cup and the fearful journey, His providence is arranging the test that will make reconciliation possible and reveal a heart transformed by grace.

crossReferences.note: Judah once proposed selling Joseph; now he offers his own freedom for a brother.
Judah's plea moves Joseph to reveal himself, the next scene of reconciliation.
The servant bearing the punishment of others, fulfilling the pattern of substitution.
Christ dying for us while we were still sinners, the substitution Judah foreshadows.

study.oneStory: The man who once sold a favored son now offers himself as a slave to save one, and to spare his father's heart. God has exposed the old guilt and brought the brothers to true repentance, healing what was broken in chapter 37. Through Judah's line the story presses toward the Messiah, and his substitution previews the cross where the innocent One takes the place of the guilty.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of hiding Joseph's cup in Benjamin's sack?

options.text: To test whether the brothers will abandon another favored son to save themselves.
options.note: The staged theft recreates the crisis of chapter 37 to prove their hearts.

options.text: To punish Benjamin for a crime he committed.
options.note: Benjamin is innocent; the cup was planted as a test, not a real theft.

options.text: To recover money the brothers had stolen.
options.note: No theft occurred; Joseph himself had the cup placed there.

options.text: To force the brothers to leave Egypt forever.
options.note: The aim is the opposite, to bring them to reconciliation.

quiz.q: How does Judah respond when the cup is found in Benjamin's sack?

options.text: He confesses that God has exposed their iniquity and offers all of them as slaves.
options.note: Judah reaches past the cup to their deeper guilt before God.

options.text: He blames Benjamin and asks to go home free.
options.note: The opposite; Judah refuses to abandon Benjamin and pleads for him.

options.text: He denies that any wrong was ever done.
options.note: He openly acknowledges guilt rather than denying it.

options.text: He accuses the steward of planting the cup.
options.note: He does not accuse anyone; he confesses guilt and pleads for mercy.

quiz.q: What does Judah finally offer to do for Benjamin?

options.text: To remain as a slave in Benjamin's place so the boy can return to their father.
options.note: Judah offers himself as a substitute, the moral summit of the account.

options.text: To pay a large ransom of silver and gold.
options.note: He offers something far costlier than money, his own freedom.

options.text: To bring their father down to Egypt as a hostage instead.
options.note: He pleads to spare their father grief, not to endanger him.

options.text: To fight the steward for Benjamin's release.
options.note: He pleads and offers himself; he does not resort to violence.

quiz.q: How does Judah's plea point forward to Christ?

options.text: The innocent one takes the place of the condemned so the beloved can go free.
options.note: This substitution is the shape of the gospel, fulfilled in Jesus of Judah's line.

options.text: It shows that eloquent speeches can change a ruler's mind.
options.note: The speech is moving, but its deeper meaning is substitution pointing to Christ.

options.text: It proves that Benjamin was secretly guilty.
options.note: Benjamin is innocent; the point is Judah standing in for him.

options.text: It has no connection to the wider biblical story.
options.note: Judah's line leads to the Messiah, and his offer prefigures the cross.

quiz.q: What change in the brothers does this chapter reveal?

options.text: They now refuse to sacrifice a favored brother, showing genuine repentance.
options.note: Where they once sold Joseph, they now stand and plead for Benjamin.

options.text: They have become wealthy and no longer care about the cost.
options.note: Their change is moral, not financial; Judah risks everything for Benjamin.

options.text: They have simply grown more afraid of Egyptian power.
options.note: Fear is present, but the deeper change is love that will not abandon a brother.

options.text: They remain exactly as callous as before.
options.note: The chapter shows how far they have come from the men of chapter 37.

questions: Judah reached past the cup to say God had exposed their guilt. Is there an old sin God has been surfacing in your life so it can finally be brought into the light?
Judah offered to trade his freedom for his brother's. What would it look like for love to cost you something real this week?
The brothers faced the same kind of test that once undid them, and this time chose differently. Where is God giving you a chance to respond rightly where you once failed?
Judah could not bear to see his father's grief. Whose good are you willing to put above your own comfort or safety?
This scene previews the innocent taking the place of the guilty. How does it deepen your wonder that Christ stood in your place?

translationNotes.note: The cup is described in terms of divination, nachash; the detail supports Joseph's Egyptian disguise, while the narrative locates his true knowledge in God's guidance.
The word for iniquity, avon, can denote guilt or the punishment guilt deserves; Judah's confession reaches beyond the cup to the brothers' deeper sin.
